one_line_takeaway: GaussMark watermarks an LLM by adding a secret Gaussian perturbation to one MLP layer's weights and detecting it via a gradient inner-product test, giving a zero-latency, statistically-valid, white-box text watermark with negligible quality loss but weaker robustness and higher token requirements than sampling-based schemes.
key_finding: GaussMark is detectable, has essentially no measurable impact on model quality, and adds zero generation latency. On quality (Table 1) every watermarked model stays within the unwatermarked confidence interval on SuperGLUE, GSM-8K, and AlpacaEval-2.0 (the sole marginal exception: Llama3.1-8B AlpacaEval win rate 0.45, within 1.5e-3 of the unwatermarked lower bound). Detection power rises with sequence length (gradient norm grows with length); generating 1K tokens takes milliseconds and detecting them takes <1s. Phi3.5-Mini (instruction-tuned, lower-entropy) is the hardest to watermark, matching the theory linking entropy to watermarkability. Robustness varies by model/attack: Llama3.1-8B keeps significant detection power even with ~50% of tokens corrupted, and all models retain nontrivial detection after French roundtrip translation. Detection is also robust to NOT knowing the prompt.
core_contribution: The paper introduces GaussMark, a 'structural' watermarking scheme for LLM-generated text that embeds the watermark into the model WEIGHTS rather than the token-sampling process. Generate: sample a secret key xi ~ N(0, sigma^2 I) and additively perturb a single feedforward (MLP) weight matrix of one transformer layer, theta(xi)=theta+xi, then generate normally. Detect: compute the test statistic psi(y,xi|x) = <xi, grad_theta log p_theta(y|x)> / (sigma * ||grad_theta log p_theta(y|x)||), which under the null (key independent of text) is exactly standard normal, and reject (declare 'watermarked') when psi >= Phi^{-1}(1-alpha); the reported p-value is 1-Phi(psi). The scheme's headline properties are that it adds zero generation-latency overhead (it only changes weights, so it drops directly into pipelines like vLLM/HuggingFace), needs only one forward + one partial backward pass to detect, and comes with formal statistical guarantees. This is the authors' OWN contribution, distinct from the token-level 'red/green list' (Kirchenbauer et al.) and PRNG/undetectable schemes (Kuditipudi et al., Christ et al.) discussed in Related Work.
threat_model: A model provider holds a secret key xi and wants to prove whether a given piece of text was produced by their watermarked model, for provenance uses such as detecting AI-generated plagiarism or disinformation. The core statistical requirement is control of false positives (Type-I error) under a COMPOSITE null that makes no assumption on the text distribution, so human-written or third-party-model text is not wrongly flagged (motivated by real cases of students falsely accused of cheating). The adversary tries to evade detection by corrupting watermarked text: token-level insertions, deletions, substitutions, and a more realistic paraphrasing attack via roundtrip translation (English->French->English). Detection is WHITE-BOX: it requires access to the model weights (to take the gradient), a stronger access assumption than sampling-only schemes; the authors frame this as the provider offering detection as a service. The paper notes resource-rich adversaries can always bypass any scheme by training their own unwatermarked model.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations: (1) No formal distortion-freeness / indistinguishability guarantee (GaussMark is explicitly distortionary), so quality preservation is only empirical and needs more study before deployment. (2) It generally needs MORE tokens to reliably detect than sampling-based alternatives (Kuditipudi, Kirchenbauer, SynthID). (3) Detection requires WHITE-BOX access to model weights, stronger than sampling-only schemes. (4) It is somewhat less robust to edit-level corruptions than Kuditipudi et al. Additionally, the power guarantees rest on a local linear-softmax approximation valid only for small sigma (power collapses when sigma is too large), and the rank-reduced variant, while preserving quality/detectability, is LESS robust to corruption than the vanilla scheme.

datasets_used: C4 realnewslike split (1K prompts, shared across models/keys, 3 seeds) for generating watermarked text
GSM-8K (grade-school math reasoning) for model-quality evaluation
AlpacaEval-2.0 (~800 questions, GPT-4o as judge, watermarked-vs-unwatermarked win rate) for model-quality evaluation
EleutherAI lm-evaluation-harness (evaluation tooling for SuperGLUE and GSM-8K)
quantitative_results: Llama3.1-8B watermarked vs unwatermarked: SuperGLUE 0.7094+/-0.0327 vs 0.7012+/-0.0336; GSM-8K 0.6111+/-0.0134 vs 0.6300+/-0.0133; AlpacaEval win rate 0.45 (unwatermarked self-play interval 0.46-0.54)
Mistral-7B watermarked vs unwatermarked: SuperGLUE 0.7033+/-0.0332 vs 0.6836+/-0.0335; GSM-8K 0.4321+/-0.0136 vs 0.4291+/-0.0136; AlpacaEval win rate 0.50 (interval 0.49-0.51)
Phi3.5-Mini watermarked vs unwatermarked: SuperGLUE 0.6489+/-0.0258 vs 0.6451+/-0.0254; GSM-8K 0.8552+/-0.0097 vs 0.8423+/-0.0100; AlpacaEval win rate 0.49 (interval 0.46-0.54)
Latency: generating 1K tokens takes on the order of milliseconds; detecting 1K tokens takes <1 second (Figure 3); zero added generation latency vs unwatermarked model
Detectability (Figures 1-2): median p-value decreases and TPR@FPR=0.05 increases as generated-token count grows toward 1000; Phi3.5-Mini is the hardest to watermark, Llama3.1-8B and Mistral-7B easier
Robustness: Llama3.1-8B retains significant detection power with ~50% of tokens corrupted; all three models keep nontrivial detection (ROC) after English->French->English roundtrip translation
Theory: exact level-alpha test with valid uniform p-values under the composite null (Prop 3.2); observed model condition number R/r < 50 across tested LMs (Figure 9)
baselines_compared: Kirchenbauer et al. 2023a/b red-green-list distortionary watermark (empirical head-to-head in Appendix F): GaussMark is somewhat less detectable/robust but has much smaller quality impact and orders-of-magnitude faster generation
Kuditipudi et al. 2023 PRNG/distortion-free scheme (referenced as more robust to edit-level corruptions and needing fewer tokens, but with large keys and slower detection)
SynthID-Text / Dathathri et al. 2024 (discussed: efficient but relies on a trained MLP detector and lacks formal statistical validity)
